user_query:
Build an event-triggered automation that continuously watches the local folder ./watch_keywords for newly added *.txt files and, for each detected file, performs a web literature sweep for eco-friendly papermaking topics, then downloads and extracts the content into a structured local archive. Use the provided sample trigger file at ./watch_keywords/example_keywords.txt to validate the workflow. Requirements:

1) Trigger and file watching:
- Monitor ./watch_keywords for files with the .txt extension. Treat this as an event-driven service that reacts when a new file is created or moved into the folder. On startup, also process any existing *.txt files.
- For each watched file, compute a SHA-256 of its entire content and record it in a state file at ./outputs/state/processed.json. Do not reprocess the same file content again if the same basename+hash pair is already present.
- Parse each non-empty line as an independent search query.

2) Internet search and filtering (must use a public web search engine without requiring credentials):
- For each query, perform a general search and collect up to 5 unique result URLs whose domains match an allowlist of official or standards-oriented sources: top-level domains .gov, .edu, .org, or the specific domains europa.eu, iso.org, and oecd.org.
- Store the final set of candidate URLs you selected for each query in the report (see 4). If no URLs match the allowlist, emit an empty result list for that query.

3) Download and content extraction:
- For every selected URL, download the resource to disk under ./outputs/raw/<keywords_file_stem>/<query_slug>/<rank>.<ext> (preserve .html or .pdf if detectable; otherwise .bin). Also compute and store a SHA-256 of the raw file.
- Extract readable text into ./outputs/text/<keywords_file_stem>/<query_slug>/<rank>.txt. For HTML, extract the visible main body text; for PDF, extract plain text; if extraction fails, create the text file containing a short error message and a zero word count.
- For each downloaded item, capture these fields for the report: url, domain, http_status (or error), content_type (best-effort), title (if available), word_count (integer of extracted text words), raw_path, text_path, sha256_raw.

4) Outputs and structure (must be created deterministically from the above steps):
- Write a JSON report for each processed keywords file at ./outputs/reports/<keywords_file_stem>.json with this structure:
  {
    "source_keywords_file": string (relative path),
    "processed_at": ISO 8601 timestamp (UTC),
    "file_sha256": SHA-256 of the keywords file content,
    "queries": [
      {
        "query": string,
        "results": [
          {
            "url": string,
            "domain": string,
            "http_status": integer or null,
            "content_type": string or null,
            "title": string or null,
            "word_count": integer,
            "raw_path": string,
            "text_path": string,
            "sha256_raw": string
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
- Append a single-line log summary per processed file to ./outputs/logs/ingest.log (include timestamp, keywords file basename, number of queries, total URLs downloaded, and any errors).
- Maintain ./outputs/state/processed.json as a JSON object mapping each processed basename to its last processed SHA-256 and processed_at time. Create the file if it does not exist.

5) General constraints:
- Do not hardcode a particular search engine brand or any fixed URLs; use a general-purpose public search engine accessible without API keys.
- Ensure the program keeps running and reacts to new files while avoiding reprocessing of the same content.
- Use only local filesystem paths under the current workspace.

Verification: After running the watcher, dropping ./watch_keywords/example_keywords.txt (already provided) should produce a JSON report at ./outputs/reports/example_keywords.json, raw and text artifacts in ./outputs/raw and ./outputs/text, a state file at ./outputs/state/processed.json, and entries in ./outputs/logs/ingest.log. The correctness will be checked via the presence and structure of these local artifacts, not specific web content. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
